eli3: Matrix decompositions factor matrices into simpler forms, revealing structure and enabling efficient computation. Think of factoring numbers: 12 = 3 × 4. For matrices: A = product of simpler matrices with nice properties.

Common decompositions: LU (for solving systems), QR (for least squares, orthogonalization), SVD (for everything — low-rank approximation, PCA, pseudo-inverse), Eigendecomposition (for powers, exponentials, dynamics).

Choosing the right decomposition depends on the problem: symmetric positive definite → Cholesky; general dense → LU or QR; rank-deficient/ill-conditioned → SVD; large sparse → iterative methods (GMRES, Conjugate Gradient).

whenWhy.title: Solving linear systems
whenWhy.body: LU: Ax = b becomes LUx = b, solve Ly = b then Ux = y. O(n³) upfront, O(n²) per RHS. Cholesky for SPD: A = LLᵀ, half the cost. Once factored, multiple RHS are cheap.

whenWhy.title: Least squares problems
whenWhy.body: QR: A = QR, solve R x = Qᵀ b. More stable than normal equations (AᵀA)⁻¹Aᵀb which squares condition number. SVD: A = UΣVᵀ, x = VΣ⁺Uᵀb gives minimum norm solution for rank-deficient A.

whenWhy.title: PCA and dimensionality reduction
whenWhy.body: SVD of data matrix X. Right singular vectors V are principal components. Σ² gives eigenvalues of covariance. Truncated SVD for low-rank approximation. Eigendecomposition of covariance XᵀX = VΛVᵀ equivalent but less stable.

whenWhy.title: Matrix functions and differential equations
whenWhy.body: Eigendecomposition A = VDV⁻¹: Aⁿ = VDⁿV⁻¹, e^A = Ve^DV⁻¹. For solving ẋ = Ax. Schur decomposition for non-diagonalizable matrices. Matrix square roots, logarithms via eigendecomposition or SVD.

deepDiveIntro: LU decomposition: A = PLU where P is permutation, L is unit lower triangular, U is upper triangular. Cholesky: A = LLᵀ for SPD A. QR: A = QR where Q orthogonal, R upper triangular. SVD: A = UΣVᵀ. Eigendecomposition: A = VDV⁻¹ for diagonalizable A. Schur: A = UTU* where U unitary, T upper triangular.

Complexity: LU and Cholesky O(n³), QR O(mn²) for m×n with m≥n, SVD O(mn²). For large sparse matrices, use iterative methods (Conjugate Gradient for SPD, GMRES for general) or sparse direct methods (supernodal, multifrontal).

deepDive.heading: LU Decomposition
deepDive.body: LU decomposition rewrites a matrix so solving a linear system becomes two simpler triangular solves. The permutation matrix captures pivoting, which is used to improve numerical stability. When the matrix is symmetric positive definite, Cholesky gives an even cheaper and cleaner factorization specialized to that structure.
deepDive.formula: Ax = b → PLUx = b → Ly = Pb → Ux = y

deepDive.heading: QR Decomposition
deepDive.body: QR decomposition separates a matrix into an orthogonal part and a triangular part. Because orthogonal transformations preserve lengths and angles, QR is especially reliable for least-squares problems and orthogonalization tasks. Stable construction methods such as Householder reflections are usually preferred in serious numerical work.

deepDive.heading: Singular Value Decomposition
deepDive.body: The singular value decomposition is the most general and versatile of the common matrix factorizations. It reveals the key input and output directions of a transformation, ranks their importance, and supports tasks such as compression, pseudo-inversion, and low-rank approximation. It is also the most robust option when matrices are rectangular, rank-deficient, or poorly conditioned.
deepDive.formula: A = UΣVᵀ, A⁺ = V diag(1/σ₁,…,1/σᵣ,0,…) Uᵀ

deepDive.heading: Eigendecomposition
deepDive.body: Eigendecomposition works when a matrix has enough eigenvectors to form a basis. In that case, many matrix functions become easy because the hard work reduces to applying the function to diagonal entries. Symmetric matrices are the nicest case, while defective matrices may require other decompositions such as Schur form.
deepDive.formula: A = VDV⁻¹, Aⁿ = VDⁿV⁻¹, e^A = Ve^DV⁻¹

deepDive.heading: Iterative Methods
deepDive.body: For very large sparse systems, forming a full direct factorization can be too expensive in memory or time. Iterative methods instead improve the solution gradually using matrix-vector products, which preserves sparsity and scales better. Their success depends heavily on preconditioning, which reshapes the problem into one that converges faster.

takeaways: LU: solving systems. Cholesky: SPD, faster.
SVD: low-rank approximation, pseudo-inverse, PCA. Most versatile.
Large sparse: iterative methods (CG, GMRES) with preconditioning.

edgeCases.q: When should I use SVD instead of eigendecomposition?
edgeCases.a: (1) Non-square matrices (SVD always exists). (2) Numerical stability: computing AᵀA squares condition number. (3) Rank-deficient matrices (SVD handles gracefully). (4) Need pseudo-inverse. Eigendecomposition is faster when A is symmetric and you know it's well-conditioned.

edgeCases.q: What happens if a matrix is not diagonalizable?
edgeCases.a: Use Jordan decomposition A = VJV⁻¹ where J is Jordan form (block diagonal with eigenvalues on diagonal, 1s on superdiagonal). Or use Schur decomposition A = UTU* which always exists. For numerical computation, Schur is preferred; Jordan is sensitive to perturbations.

edgeCases.q: When do iterative methods beat direct methods?
edgeCases.a: For large sparse matrices. Direct methods (LU, Cholesky) suffer fill-in — sparse A becomes dense L and U. Iterative methods only need matrix-vector products, preserving sparsity. CG converges fast if condition number is small or eigenvalues are clustered. For 3D PDEs, iterative often win when n > 10⁴.

sources.label: Golub & Van Loan — Matrix Computations (4th ed.)
